news_summary: A leading European science funder has reversed stricter regulations following researchers' concerns, reflecting a positive response to community feedback and support for academic freedom.
news_summary_fr: Un important organisme européen de financement de la recherche a annulé des mesures réglementaires plus strictes à la suite des préoccupations exprimées par les chercheurs, ce qui témoigne d'une réponse positive aux commentaires de la communauté et d'un soutien à la liberté académique.
news_summary_es: Una de las principales entidades europeas de financiación de la investigación científica ha revocado una normativa más estricta tras las preocupaciones expresadas por los investigadores, lo que refleja una respuesta positiva a las opiniones de la comunidad y un apoyo a la libertad académica.

articles.title: Prestigious European science funder scraps stricter rules after researcher backlash
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01435-6The tough new policy was introduced to curb submissions, but drew criticism from researchers.
articles.content:
The European Research Council proposed the rule change in response to an increasing volume of grant applications.Credit: Tom Werner/Getty

The European Research Council (ERC) has reversed a policy it introduced to help deal with a deluge of applications to its coveted awards, after a huge researcher backlash against the measure.

On 16 April the ERC, Europe’s most prestigious funder of basic research, said it would tighten restrictions on how soon researchers could reapply after failing to secure funding, to help peer reviewers to cope with growing demand and a static annual budget of around €2.3 billion (US$2.66 billion).

European funder must increase capacity to meet the ambition of scientists

But many researchers strongly opposed the changes, which might have meant some unsuccessful applicants having to wait up to 4 years before they could apply for funding again. The council’s plan was “3000% the wrong direction”, wrote Anthony Guihur, a molecular biologist at the healthcare company Labcorp, in Geneva, Switzerland, on the social media platform Bluesky. An open letter that quickly gathered more than 1,000 signatories argued that longer exclusion periods risked discouraging bold ideas and pushing talent away from Europe.

In a statement published on 29 April, the ERC scientific council, which decides the body’s funding and management strategy, said it had “listened to the concerns” of the research community and would reverse the changes.

The ERC’s swift response and willingness to listen to feedback is reassuring, says Johanna Joyce, a cancer biologist and geneticist at the University of Lausanne in Switzerland, and one of the organizers of the open letter. “But the underlying pressure on the system remains real.”

Grant deluge

The ERC is not alone in seeing a surge in applications for grants. At least a dozen funders have seen submissions rise since 2022, which many suspect is the result of researchers using artificial intelligence models to write applications more easily.

Applications for the ERC’s Advanced Grants, for example, rose by 31% between 2024 and 2025 with a particular growth in interest from US-based researchers.

The ERC’s peer review system is struggling to handle the flood of applications. Peer review panels, which meet to evaluate proposals, have to deal with more than 250 proposals each, up from 50-150 in the past, said the council’s president Maria Leptin in a letter on 16 April.

Is UK science in jeopardy? Huge funding reforms spark chaos and anxiety

Despite streamlining the evaluation procedure and using “soft measures” to reduce applications, the council felt the need to introduce a more direct policy, she said. Under the new rules, researchers who received a “B” score at the first stage of the application process for any of the three main grant programmes in 2026 or 2025 would be barred from applying again in 2027, with the ban extending to 2024 for unsuccessful applicants scoring the lowest “C” grade. Until now, researchers had to wait just one year for a B score or two years for a C, before being able to apply again.
articles.reasoning: The European Research Council reversed a restrictive funding policy after strong researcher opposition, preserving fair access to research grants and encouraging bold scientific ideas. This decision positively impacts the broader scientific community across Europe and beyond by maintaining equitable opportunities for funding, which supports innovation and talent retention.
articles.personality_title: European Research Council reverses strict grant rules after researcher protests
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Research Council (ERC) is a major science funder in Europe. Recently, it faced many more applications for research grants than before, making it hard for reviewers to manage. To handle this, the ERC planned to make rules stricter about when researchers could apply again after being turned down.

**What happened** – In April 2026, the ERC announced new rules that would force some researchers to wait up to four years before applying again if they did not get funding. Many scientists disagreed strongly, saying this would stop new and bold ideas and push talent away from Europe. Over 1,000 researchers signed an open letter against the changes. After hearing these concerns, the ERC decided on April 29 to cancel the new waiting period rules and keep the previous, shorter waiting times.

**Impact** – This decision keeps the funding process fair and open, allowing researchers to try again sooner if they don't get a grant. It helps encourage creative and risky projects that might lead to important scientific discoveries. It also shows that the ERC listens to the research community and values their input.

**What's next step** – The ERC still faces many more grant applications than before, partly because tools like artificial intelligence make it easier to write proposals. The council will need to find new ways to manage the high demand for funding without limiting opportunities for researchers. Experts say this challenge will continue as science grows.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After strong feedback from scientists, the European Research Council reversed tough grant rules to keep funding accessible and support innovative research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Conseil européen de la recherche annule des règles strictes sur les subventions après la contestation des chercheurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Conseil européen de la recherche (CER) est un important financeur scientifique en Europe. Récemment, il a reçu beaucoup plus de demandes de subventions, ce qui a rendu difficile la gestion pour les évaluateurs. Pour y faire face, le CER avait prévu d'assouplir les règles sur le délai de nouvelle candidature après un refus.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En avril 2026, le CER a annoncé de nouvelles règles obligeant certains chercheurs à attendre jusqu'à quatre ans avant de pouvoir postuler à nouveau en cas de refus. De nombreux scientifiques se sont fortement opposés, affirmant que cela freinerait les idées nouvelles et pousserait les talents hors d'Europe. Plus de 1 000 chercheurs ont signé une lettre ouverte contre ces changements. Après avoir entendu ces préoccupations, le CER a décidé le 29 avril d'annuler ces règles et de conserver les délais plus courts.

**Impact** – Cette décision maintient un accès juste et ouvert au financement, permettant aux chercheurs de retenter leur chance plus rapidement. Elle encourage les projets créatifs et audacieux qui pourraient mener à des découvertes importantes. Elle montre aussi que le CER écoute la communauté scientifique et prend en compte son avis.

**Prochaine étape** – Le CER doit toujours gérer un nombre croissant de demandes, en partie grâce aux outils d'intelligence artificielle qui facilitent la rédaction des propositions. Le conseil devra trouver de nouvelles solutions pour gérer cette forte demande sans limiter les opportunités des chercheurs. Ce défi devrait se poursuivre avec le développement de la science.

**Phrase-clé** – Après un fort retour des scientifiques, le Conseil européen de la recherche a annulé des règles strictes pour garder un accès équitable aux financements et soutenir la recherche innovante.
articles.personality_title_es: El Consejo Europeo de Investigación revierte reglas estrictas de subvenciones tras protestas de investigadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Consejo Europeo de Investigación (ERC) es un importante financiador científico en Europa. Recientemente, recibió muchas más solicitudes de subvenciones, lo que dificultó la gestión para los evaluadores. Para enfrentar esto, el ERC planeó hacer más estrictas las reglas sobre cuándo los investigadores podían volver a solicitar después de ser rechazados.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, el ERC anunció reglas nuevas que obligarían a algunos investigadores a esperar hasta cuatro años para volver a solicitar si no obtenían financiamiento. Muchos científicos se opusieron fuertemente, diciendo que esto frenaría ideas nuevas y valientes y alejaría talento de Europa. Más de 1,000 investigadores firmaron una carta abierta contra los cambios. Después de escuchar estas preocupaciones, el ERC decidió el 29 de abril cancelar las nuevas reglas y mantener los tiempos de espera más cortos anteriores.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión mantiene el proceso de financiamiento justo y abierto, permitiendo a los investigadores intentarlo de nuevo más pronto si no obtienen una subvención. Ayuda a fomentar proyectos creativos y arriesgados que pueden llevar a importantes descubrimientos científicos. También muestra que el ERC escucha a la comunidad científica y valora sus opiniones.

**Próximo paso** – El ERC sigue enfrentando muchas más solicitudes de subvenciones, en parte porque herramientas como la inteligencia artificial facilitan la redacción de propuestas. El consejo necesitará encontrar nuevas formas de manejar la alta demanda sin limitar las oportunidades para los investigadores. Se espera que este desafío continúe con el crecimiento de la ciencia.

**Frase clave** – Tras fuertes comentarios de los científicos, el Consejo Europeo de Investigación revirtió reglas estrictas para mantener el acceso al financiamiento y apoyar la investigación innovadora.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, ornate balance scale symbolizing fairness and decision-making, with one side holding glowing, stylized scientific research papers and the other side gently lifted by a diverse group of silhouetted, abstract figures representing researchers united in dialogue, set against a soft, neutral background that suggests openness and collaboration.
